content: I want to make a really good spaghetti carbonara tonight. What's the secret to getting the sauce silky instead of scrambled?

The trick is temperature control. Whisk the eggs and grated Pecorino together off the heat, then toss them with the hot pasta away from the burner, adding a splash of starchy pasta water to loosen it. The residual heat cooks the eggs into a creamy sauce without scrambling them.
Should I use guanciale or pancetta, and do I need cream?

You're making a classic Italian dish, I presume? Both guanciale and pancetta are excellent choices, but they have slightly different flavor profiles. Guanciale has a more delicate, unsmoked flavor, while pancetta is often smoked and has a bolder taste. If you want a more authentic carbonara experience, guanciale might be the way to go. But if you can't find guanciale or prefer a stronger flavor, pancetta is a great substitute.

As for the cream, traditional carbonara recipes actually don't include any cream. The creaminess comes from the eggs, cheese, and pasta starch. Adding cream can make the dish richer, but it can also make it feel heavier and less authentic. If you want to keep it traditional, you can omit the cream and focus on cooking the eggs and pasta just right to create a creamy sauce. That being said, if you're looking for a more indulgent take on carbonara, a small amount of cream can enhance the flavor and texture. What's your personal preference?

content:
Guanciale can be a bit tricky to find, but it's definitely worth the hunt. I've had luck finding it at specialty Italian markets or high-end grocery stores that carry a wide selection of cured meats. If you're having trouble finding it in stores, you could also try looking online at Italian import stores or butcher shops that ship nationwide. Some stores may also carry pancetta, which is similar to guanciale but made from pork belly instead of jowl. While it's not the same, pancetta can be a good substitute in a pinch.

As for using bacon as a substitute, it's definitely doable, but it will change the flavor profile of the dish. Bacon has a smokier, more pronounced flavor than guanciale, which can be a bit overpowering in carbonara. If you do decide to use bacon, I'd recommend using a thinner-cut, more delicate bacon to try to get closer to the flavor of guanciale.

In terms of pasta, spaghetti is a classic choice for carbonara, and it works beautifully. The long, thin strands of spaghetti allow the creamy sauce to coat the pasta evenly, and it's easy to twirl around your fork. Bucatini is also a great choice, and it's actually a more traditional pairing in some parts of Italy. The hollow center of the bucatini allows the sauce to get trapped inside, which can be really delicious. Rigatoni, on the other hand, is a bit of an unconventional choice for carbonara, but it can still work well if you're looking for a shorter, more rustic pasta shape. Ultimately, the type of pasta you choose will depend on your personal preference, but I think spaghetti or bucatini are the way to go for a classic carbonara.

One thing to keep in mind, regardless of the pasta you choose, is to make sure you're cooking it al dente. This will help the pasta hold onto the sauce better, and it'll also give the dish a nicer texture overall. Have you thought about what kind of cheese you'll be using in your carbonara? Parmigiano-Reggiano is the classic choice, but some people also like to use Pecorino Romano for a sharper, more pronounced flavor.

The lower protein content and finer texture make it ideal for creating delicate, tender noodles. I've also experimented with ancient grains like Kamut and spelt, and I've found that they add a wonderful nutty flavor and texture to the pasta. Kamut, in particular, has a slightly sweet and buttery flavor that pairs beautifully with rich and creamy sauces.

As for pairing flours with ingredients and sauces, I've found that "00" flour is very versatile and can be paired with a wide range of ingredients, from light and delicate sauces to rich and hearty ones. Kamut and spelt, on the other hand, seem to pair better with earthier ingredients like mushrooms, truffles, and game meats. As for drying and storing fresh pasta, I usually dry it on a pasta drying rack or a clean towel, and then store it in an airtight container in the fridge for up to a day or two. If I'm not planning to use it immediately, I'll often freeze it and then thaw it as needed.

As for making unusual pasta shapes, I've had some experience with trofie and bigoli. Trofie can be a bit tricky to make, as they require a very specific texture and shape. I've found that using a combination of "00" flour and water, and then kneading the dough for a long time, helps to create the right texture. Bigoli, on the other hand, are a bit easier to make, but they do require a bit of practice to get the shape just right. I've found that using a bigoli cutter or a pastry bag with a large round tip can help to create the right shape. One tip I would give is to make sure that the dough is at the right consistency, not too sticky and not too dry, and to use a light touch when shaping the pasta. It's also important to work quickly, as the pasta can start to dry out and become brittle if it's exposed to air for too long.

One thing I've found is that it's really important to have the right filling consistency - if it's too wet, it can be hard to seal the pasta properly, and if it's too dry, it can be difficult to get the filling to distribute evenly. I've also found that using a combination of cheeses, like Parmesan and ricotta, can add a lot of depth and complexity to the filling.

As for the pasta itself, I've found that using a thinner sheet of pasta is better for filled pasta, as it's easier to seal and less likely to tear. I've also developed a little trick for sealing the pasta - I use a tiny bit of water to moisten the edge of the pasta, and then I use a fork to press the edges together. It's amazing how well this works, and it's really helped me to get a good seal on my filled pasta.

I've also experimented with different types of fillings, like butternut squash and sage, or mushroom and truffle. These flavors pair really well with the earthier flavors of the Kamut and spelt flours, and they add a lot of interest and complexity to the pasta. I've even tried making some more unusual fillings, like pumpkin and amaretti, or pear and gorgonzola. These flavors might sound a bit strange, but they actually work really well together, and they add a lot of excitement to the pasta.

One thing I've been wanting to try is making filled pasta with a more delicate filling, like lobster or crab.
